era_context: Identical to -2354 and -2353: Old Kingdom Egypt in terminal decline, Akkadian Empire at zenith under Naram-Sin, Indus Valley Civilization in mature phase, Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Anatolia.

events.title: Egyptian Old Kingdom: administrative collapse accelerates; nomarchs consolidate regional power
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty's authority continues to erode. Provincial governors (nomarchs) in Upper Egypt increasingly act as independent rulers, controlling local resources, maintaining private armies, and commissioning their own monumental tombs. The pharaoh's ability to levy taxes and conscript labor diminishes. Some scholars suggest climate stress (reduced Nile inundation) exacerbates the administrative crisis. The transition to the First Intermediate Period is imminent, though the exact year of the dynasty's formal end is uncertain.
key_figures: Pepi II (Pharaoh, if still reigning) or his immediate successor

sources.name: Autobiographical inscriptions of nomarchs (Ankhtifi, Siese, Kheti)

events.certainty_note: The exact chronology of the Old Kingdom's collapse is debated. The year -2352 is assigned to this period by convention, but the specific events of this year are not documented.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: military campaigns in the Zagros and Levant; consolidation of divine kingship ideology
events.description: Naram-Sin continues military campaigns to suppress rebellions and maintain control over distant provinces. He campaigns in the Zagros Mountains against the Lullubi and other highland peoples. In the Levant, he consolidates control over city-states and trade routes. His inscriptions increasingly emphasize his divine status and military prowess. The empire remains at its territorial maximum, but internal strains and external pressures are beginning to accumulate.
events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-documented, but specific year-to-year military campaigns are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: continued stability and standardized production
events.description: The mature Harappan civilization continues without apparent disruption. Urban centers maintain their planned layouts and standardized infrastructure. Craft production, including pottery, seals, and metal work, continues at established standards. Trade networks remain active. The civilization shows remarkable continuity and stability, with no evidence of major political upheaval, warfare, or environmental crisis during this period.
events.certainty_note: The mature Harappan phase is archaeologically well-established. Year-to-year variation is not distinguishable from the archaeological record.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for -2352 identified. The patterns described are consistent with the broader historical trajectory of this period.
historiographic_note: The year -2352 remains undocumented in specific terms. The entries describe the general historical context and trends. Ancient chronologies for the third millennium BCE are uncertain to within ±5–10 years, and year-to-year documentation is rare. The historical record for this year is essentially identical to -2354 and -2353.
